topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my screen, a sterile little alert announcing the publication. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake of air that felt like swallowing glass. I clicked the link, and there it was: my piece, meticulously crafted, my voice poured onto the digital page, but the byline was wrong. It was someone else’s name, a stranger’s name, plastered over my hard-won words. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my hands tremble so violently I had to grip the edge of my desk. I reread the text, the familiar sentences now tainted by this blatant appropriation. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small room, my jaw tight. The air felt suddenly thick, suffocating. "This is unacceptable," I muttered, the words raspy, laced with a cold, hard fury. I began deleting the draft, the cursor blinking mockingly on the screen, each deletion a small act of severance from the intrusion.

[story 2]

I stared at the printed proof spread across the table. The typeface was right, the arguments were mine, yet the signature at the bottom belonged to a complete unknown. A slow, chilling sensation began to spread from my chest outward, a heavy, unpleasant pressure. I picked up the paper, the texture of the cheap newsprint rough beneath my fingertips. My knuckles turned white as I held it steady, fighting the urge to crumple it into a tight, angry ball. Every line felt like a betrayal. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent city traffic, the noise a dull, irritating hum against the roaring silence in my head. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to scrub the unfamiliar name from my vision, but it just swam there, mocking me. When I finally opened them, the desire to smash the evidence seemed overwhelming, a physical need to destroy the thing that had been stolen.

[story 3]

It took three full minutes for the realization to fully sink in. I leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning under my weight, and let the silence stretch until it became unbearable. When I finally looked at the final version, the air seemed to evacuate my lungs. My stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant knot forming just beneath my ribs. I felt a profound sense of violation, as if someone had reached into my private space and rearranged the furniture without permission. I didn't speak immediately; instead, I simply folded the document, slowly, deliberately, as if handling something contaminated. My movements were stiff, precise, betraying the internal turmoil raging within. I pushed the papers away, the sound barely audible, and began methodically gathering my notes, every gesture radiating a brittle, contained resentment.
